data.title: BankUnited Isn't Ready For An Upgrade, Even As Earnings Near
data.description:
Ever since early 2022, I have dedicated more time to the financial sector. It was always a glaring hole in my portfolio of industries in terms of understanding. But given the banking crisis that occurred back then, I felt as though it was high time to jump in with both feet. One of the banks that I eventually came across was BankUnited (BKU), a rather sizable institution with a market capitalization today of $3.40 billion. Although this does not make it anywhere near a giant in the banking sector, it is actually larger than most of the banks that assess. Unfortunately, I have not been able to be bullish on the business. In my last article about it, published in July of 2025, I reaffirmed it as a ‘hold’ candidate. But since then, the stock has outperformed the market, jumping 19.4% while the S&P 500 is up 10.6%.

The most recent data that we have today helps us to understand exactly why the stock moved up so much. Although certain asset quality metrics remain disappointing, shares are not terribly expensive. The balance sheet and the income statement both continue to grow as well. And just around the corner, the company will be announcing financial results covering the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. Leading up to that point, revenue is expected to rise, though it does look as though earnings might pull back slightly. Unfortunately, especially in this space, I am very picky about the companies I turn bullish on. And while there are some positive things for us to enjoy about it, the stock is not cheap enough to justify a bullish outlook at this time, even though the market has so far disagreed with my overall view since my last article about it.

I’m not ready to bank on BankUnited

When it comes to banks, I always like to start with the balance sheet. And my favorite aspect of that to look at would be deposits. Even though these are liabilities, they serve as the literal fuel for the growth of these enterprises. And growth is one thing that we have seen as of late. In the third quarter of 2025, BankUnited boasted deposits of $28.62 billion. This was up from the $27.87 billion the company had at the end of 2024. One thing that I do find to be impressive here is that this increase in deposits came even as broker deposits for the institution dropped, falling from $5.2 billion to $4.5 billion. These are high cost deposits borrowed from other financial institutions. So to see a decline here in and of itself is positive, especially if organic deposit growth was strong enough to push overall deposits higher.

I should also point out that the growth in deposits that BankUnited experienced during this window came about despite the fact that public funds deposits dropped precipitously from $3.1 billion to $1.9 billion. This kind of volatility when it comes to public funds isn't shocking. They are highly seasonal in nature, but what is shocking is what this means for overall organic deposit growth. It basically means that the picture for the company improved markedly during this time.

A growth in deposits allows an increase in the asset side of the balance sheet also. But surprisingly, loans, which are the main area you would expect to see some expansion, did not experience any. In fact, from the end of 2024 to the third quarter of 2025, the value of loans dropped from $24.07 billion to $23.48 billion. The greatest exposure that the institution has when it comes to loans would be the $6.61 billion of commercial and industrial loans on its books. However, it also has a lot of money tied up into one to four family residential properties as well as under the non-owner-occupied commercial real estate category. These amounts totaled $6.20 billion and $5.82 billion, respectively.

Even though loans didn't increase, other parts of the balance sheet did. The company went from having $9.34 billion of securities to having $9.63 billion worth. Cash and cash equivalents did increase from $491.1 million to $559.5 million. But the big change involved debt. Management has been working to restructure the composition of the balance sheet. The whole objective here has been to boost profitability. nd one of the easiest ways to tackle that is to use low cost or no cost deposits to pay off debt. Sure enough, the company did just that, reducing debt from $3.64 billion at the end of 2024 to $2.40 billion in the third quarter of 2025.

These changes to the composition of the balance sheet, as well as changes to the interest rate environment more broadly, have had a positive impact on the income statement for the institution. The company went from generating $224.9 million of net interest income in the third quarter of 2024 to generating $238.5 million worth in the third quarter of 2025. This was in spite of the fact that the provision for credit losses grew from $9.2 million to $11.6 million. It appears as though one driver ended up being an increase in the company's net interest margin from 2.78% to 3%. It is worth noting that, as interest rates decline, the amount that banks have to pay to depositors can drop immediately. So a declining interest rate environment, as contrary as it might sound, can be a net positive for institutions such as BankUnited.

Non-interest income also grew, climbing from $22.9 million to $25.6 million. This was primarily the result of other non-interest income items soaring from $11.5 million to $16 million. Management did not really provide any detail here. But they did say that this category includes such items as lending related fees other than origination fees, syndication fees, bank owned life insurance income, securities gains and losses, foreign currency fees, and commercial card revenue. Regardless, it is nice to see an increase here.

This growth on the top line pushed net profits up to $71.9 million. That's compared to the $61.5 million that the institution reported the previous year. And for the first nine months of 2025, net profits amounted to $199.1 million. The year earlier, it was $163.2 million. Overall, this is an encouraging trend. And if we project out results based on what we have seen already for 2025, we would have an institution that's trading at a price to earnings multiple of 12.5. In the chart above, you can see that this puts the company tied as the cheapest between it and five institutions that I stacked it up against. But honestly, I prefer institutions in the financial sector that are trading at price to earnings multiples of 10 or lower. So even though the company is cheap on a relative basis, it's not cheap enough for my liking.

In the chart above, you can see the price to book and price to tangible book multiples of the institution compared to the same five companies I stacked it up against already. On this basis, things actually look better. I would prefer that at least the price to book multiple be less than 1. But it's not terribly higher, and it's around the low end of the spectrum compared to these other comparable institutions. If only asset quality was higher, I would be more optimistic. But that's sadly not the case.

In the chart above, you can see the return on assets reading of BankUnited compared to the same five banks. I typically like to see a reading here of 1% or greater. But unfortunately, BankUnited stands at 0.82%. This also puts it around the middle of the group, with two of the five comparable firms worse off than it is. In the chart below, I then compared it to the same three institutions when it comes to the return on equity reading. In this case, three of the institutions are worse off than it is. But the 9.50% reading that we get here is just a hair below the 10% minimum that I prefer.

Another thing to take into consideration here is credit quality. And this is something I really don't like about the bank. Typically, I like to see no more than 0.50% of all assets classified as non-performing. The same holds true for loans. But as of the end of the third quarter, the readings for the institution were 1.10% and 1.60%, respectively. This is quite a bit above what I prefer. And that certainly works as a knock against it.

Of course, my opinion could change as new data comes in. And it just so happens that, on January 21st, the management team at BankUnited will be announcing financial results for the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. The current expectation is for revenue of $279.8 million. This should refer to the sum of net interest income before the provision for credit losses and non-interest income. Fortunately, for the final quarter of 2025, BankUnited had a reading of $264.5 million. So if everything goes according to plan, it will see some growth here. But sadly, analysts believe that earnings per share will contract from $0.91 to $0.89, which would drop net profits from $69.3 million to $66.6 million.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I believe that while BankUnited is an interesting prospect, it's not one that I am enthusiastic enough to be bullish about. If we could see some changes when it comes to asset quality and credit quality, and if the stock could be a bit cheaper, my mindset would likely change. That could happen if the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year comes in strong. But for now, I believe that maintaining the institution as a ‘hold’ is the right choice.

data.title: Oil Price Volatility Is Our Base Case In A Visibly Surplus Market
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Massif Capital Q4 2025 Letter To Investors.

Oil and Natural Gas (16% of Portfolio, 3 Positions)

Oil price volatility in 2026 is our base case, and it appears less an aberration than a consequence of two forces that coexist uncomfortably: a visibly surplus market and an episodic geopolitical premium. On paper, the surplus is material, forecasts reach as high as 2.3 mb/d, yet prices remain hostage to recurrent geopolitical risk. Each recalibration of risk around the United States, Russia, Iran, or Venezuela temporarily lifts prices, even as the Oil Price and Geopolitical Events (2025 and 2026) underlying supply-and-demand arithmetic deteriorates. The pattern is familiar. In 2025, oil declined year over year, yet repeatedly spiked amid supply scares that proved transient.

What is less evident is a mechanism that would carry prices decisively higher or lower on an annual basis. Low OECD inventories and geopolitical fragility argue for a floor; rising non-OPEC supply via the arrival of the last wave of major post-COVID projects argues for a ceiling. Final investment decisions taken between 2020 and 2022, particularly offshore, are now converting into first oil across Brazil, Guyana, Norway, and the Gulf of Mexico. Unfortunately, there is scant evidence that oil majors have replenished the development queue with comparable new projects. The result is a market that likely clears excess barrels early in the year, then steadies as attention drifts toward 2027 and beyond when supply looks less robust.

Morgan Stanley (MS) frames the same outcome more mechanically. In The Oil Manual: Outlook 202c – Letting the Curve Do the Work, the bank argues that adjustment will occur through structure rather than level: rising inventories pressure the front of the Brent curve into contango while longer-dated prices remain comparatively anchored. Spot prices, in this telling, find support in the mid-$50s, not low enough to force capitulation, not high enough to revive enthusiasm. Volatility persists, direction does not.

Even geopolitical disruptions struggle to alter that oil trajectory sustainably. Severe outcomes in Iran or Venezuela would likely manifest as short squeezes rather than regime changes, their impact absorbed by an already ample surplus. By 2027, oil will have spent more than two years in retreat, importantly, not because demand failed to appear, but because supply overshot robust demand. That distinction matters. Prior sub-$60 episodes in the past decade, 2016 and 2018 to 2019, were resolved not by demand recovery but by investment withdrawal, a process that only reveals itself with a lag.

On that point, the numbers are unambiguous. The International Energy Agency estimates that, absent new investment, global oil production declines by roughly 5.5 mb/d annually, more than the combined output of Brazil and Norway. The US Energy Information Administration estimates demand growth of approximately 1.4 mb/d in 2026. The implication is a replacement requirement approaching 6.9 mb/d. Yet capital spending fell an estimated 5–6% last year, the first contraction since 2020. Those cuts do not bite immediately; they surface after a delay of 6 to 18 months. By the second half of 2026, the surplus that defines the year may already be eroding, quietly setting the conditions for a very different debate.

In this context, we believe our portfolio is well-positioned. If the market in 2026 is defined less by scarcity than by interruption, investment choices matter more than directional conviction. We therefore continue to favor companies whose economics are already proven at mid-cycle prices, whose capital has largely been spent, and whose balance sheets do not require the indulgence of a cooperative spot market. In a regime where Brent oscillates rather than trends, durability should command a premium over optionality.

Vår Energi (VARRY) is the most direct expression of this bias. Its cash flow profile is no longer prospective but operational, with the Johan Castberg and Balder X fields transitioning from promise to production. We estimate that in a $55 to $70 price range VAR has the highest free-cash-flow yield in the European E&P space, with a free-cash-flow yield of as much as 18%, a statistic that owes more to capex flexibility than to any heroic oil price assumption. The company’s ability to modulate spending while preserving distributions leaves it exposed to volatility, but not hostage to it.

Harbour Energy (PMOIF) complements this exposure in a different way. Harbour’s near-term uplift in free cash flow is driven less by oil prices than by balance-sheet and portfolio decisions, most notably the Waldorf and LLOG transactions, which transform the company’s cash generation across a $55–70/bbl Brent range. Under such conditions, we expect incremental free cash flow of $100–200 million in 2026, effectively doubling cumulative generation relative to the standalone base. In a surplus market punctuated by episodic geopolitical spikes, this distinction, managerial skill over commodity beta, matters.

Finally, Equinor (EQNR) provides ballast. With scale, diversification, and a net-cash balance sheet, Equinor’s exposure is skewed toward late-cycle offshore supply that has already cleared the sanctioning hurdle. Its familiarity with the Norwegian Continental Shelf and preference for infrastructure-led developments mean that near-term volumes are largely embedded in the forward-looking plan, reducing reliance on fresh final investment decisions at precisely the moment when the industry’s appetite for them appears to be waning. The common thread is that returns are being earned through past decisions rather than future oil price hopes. Should Morgan Stanley’s expectation of a contangoed curve, weak prompt pricing set against more resilient long-dated prices, materialize, and should volatility remain the dominant feature of 2026, the advantage should accrue to producers with high free-cash-flow yields, flexible capital programs, and balance-sheet slack. In such a market, the question is not who benefits most from a rally, but who can thrive in its absence.

data.title: PPA: This Defense ETF's Massive 2025 Outperformance Could Continue
data.description:
After delivering one of the best returns in 2025 and beating the S&P 500 by 125%, Invesco Aerospace & Defense ETF’s (PPA) share price surge accelerated in 2026 due to the global geopolitical tensions and higher defense spending. In addition, the defense companies backlog and financial outlook also indicate a robust and long-lasting growth trend ahead. Therefore, it is still a great time to invest in the defense industry through investment vehicles, such as Invesco Aerospace & Defense ETF (PPA). I maintain my buy rating on PPA.

PPA’s 2025 Performance

Fiscal 2025 was one of the best years for defense investors, supported by the global geopolitical tensions. In addition, a global approach of equipping armies with the latest defense technologies boosted the demand trend. Over the past few years, the technological innovations have changed war strategies. Instead of gathering large armies, countries are now relying on unmanned drones, jets, missiles and other technologies for accuracy.

The growth trend has also been reflecting in defense-focused securities performance. The Invesco Aerospace & Defense ETF, which tracks the performance of companies systemically important to defense and security, delivered 36% in price return in 2025 compared the S&P 500’s 16% upside. In 2024 and 2023, PPA’s price returns were 24% and 18%, respectively.

The Potential Upside in 2026

PPA’s price momentum is likely to accelerate in 2026 due to the prospects of a further boost in defense spending across the world. In particular, United States, the largest defense spender, suggested 2027 military budget around $1.5 trillion, significantly higher compared to $901 billion approved for 2026.

The escalating tensions between US and Iran, increasing stress among Saudi Arabia and UAE and ongoing Russia-Ukraine conflict contributes to defense demand. In addition, tensions between Israel and Middle Eastern countries and challenging geopolitical conditions in the Asia Pacific region have also been adding to an unprecedented demand. European region also seeks to steadily reach their defense spending target of 5% of GDP.

Overall, the defense segment’s fundamentals seem significantly strong. Therefore, despite a blistering share prices growth in the past few years, it still appears to be a great time to invest in the industry through investment vehicles, such as Invesco Aerospace & Defense ETF. Year to date, PPA's share price surged 15%. I expect PPA to deliver higher returns in 2026 compared to 2025.

PPA’s Portfolio Holdings Indicate Robust and Long-Lasting Growth

Invesco Aerospace & Defense ETF tracks SPADE Defense Index by using full replication technique. PPA's portfolio comprises 62 defense stocks. The fund’s top 10 holdings make-up 59% of its portfolio weight. Boeing (BA), RTX (RTX), Lockheed Martin (LMT), GE Aerospace (GE) and Northrop Grumman Corp (NOC) are its top five holdings. All these companies generated a blistering growth in the last twelve months amid their record profitability and massive backlogs.

For example, Boeing's share price rallied 14% year to date and 46% in the last twelve months. In the latest quarter, the company’s backlog grew to $636 billion. Whereas, RTX Corp experienced sales growth of 12% and its adjusted earnings per share increased 17% compared to the prior year. It ended the quarter with a backlog of $251 billion, representing 13% increase year-over-year. The company’s share price soared 10% year to date and 67% in the last twelve months.

Similarly, GE Aerospace, Lockheed Martin and Northrop Grumman reported an unprecedented demand trend. Lockheed Martin, the fighter jet maker, anticipates that its record $179 billion backlog is equivalent to more than two and a half years of its sales. Therefore, the company seeks to increase its operational efficiencies to meet the demand trend. Here is what its CEO stated:

Based on the effectiveness and reliability of our products and systems, strong demand from Lockheed Martin's customers—both in the United States and among our allies—continues. As a result of this unprecedented demand, we are increasing production capacity significantly across a wide range of our lines of business

PPA’s next five holdings are General Dynamics (GD), L3Harris Technologies (LHX), Honeywell International (HON), Howmet Aerospace (HWM) and Parker Hannifin (PH). All these companies have also delivered solid share price growth on the back of robust demand trend.

In the latest quarter, General Dynamics reported 10.6% revenue and 15.8% earnings per share growth from the year-ago quarter. The company claims its order activity was very strong as its total estimated contract value reached $167.7 billion. Howmet Aerospace, which generated 14% revenue and 17% earning growth in the latest quarter, anticipates strong commercial and defense demand in 2026.

PPA’s Interesting Characteristics

Invesco Aerospace & Defense ETF’s other key characteristics include a solid liquidity. PPA’s average shares trading volume hovers around 200K, up from the median of all ETFs of 37K. Its assets under management are around $8 billion. The fund also lifted its dividend in the last two consecutive years. In 2026, a hefty dividend hike is expected, given the robust earnings growth power of its portfolio holdings. The fund’s expense ratio of 0.58% is slightly higher from the median of all ETFs of 0.50%.

Other Players Also Offer Lofty Returns

Other popular options in the aerospace & defense industry include iShares U.S. Aerospace & Defense ETF (ITA), State Street® SPDR® S&P® Aerospace & Defense ETF (XAR) and Global X Defense Tech ETF (SHLD). All three ETFs generated a blistering growth in the last twelve months. In particular, SHLD’s price rally of nearly 100% indicates the robust demand for defense technology. The fund seeks to track the performance of companies with a greater exposure to defense technologies.

Whereas, iShares U.S. Aerospace & Defense ETF mainly targets US based aerospace & defense companies. It seeks to track the performance of the Dow Jones U.S. Select Aerospace & Defense Index by using representative sampling technique. Its portfolio comprises 46 stocks. On the other hand, State Street® SPDR® S&P® Aerospace & Defense ETF offers a more targeted exposure to the US aerospace & defense industry. Its portfolio is make-up of 41 stocks.

Risk Factors

The aerospace & defense industry is exposed to various risk factors. The risk of tariffs, regulatory challenges and economic trends can negatively impact its performance. As the United States is the largest exporter of defense & aerospace products, the higher tariffs and regulatory challenges can impact the industry’s performance. In addition, a recession can also create challenges for the industry.

Final Thoughts

As demand in defense category is expected to accelerate in 2026, it is still a great time to capitalize on potential opportunities. Invesco Aerospace & Defense ETF is one of the best options with exposure to defense category. Its portfolio comprises a large number of holdings. The fund’s price momentum is robust, liquidity is strong and dividend growth is healthy. Whereas, the defense technology investors can also consider ETFs like SHLD. ITA and XAR could also be intersting options to gain a targeted exposure to US aerospace & defense industry.

data.title: Wolverine World Wide: Shares Are Getting Close To Being On Sale
data.description:
Honestly, I have never been much of a big fan of the clothing and apparel market. It is highly competitive, low margin, and subject to fickle consumer preferences. But there has always been one segment in this market that has appealed to me. And that is none other than the footwear space. Some of the companies in this market are attractive in doing exceptionally well. Others are doing fine, but not great. And even the ones that are doing fine don't necessarily make for attractive opportunities. One firm that I have been more neutral on as of late is Wolverine World Wide, Inc. (WWW), an enterprise that focuses on casual footwear and apparel, performance outdoor and athletic footwear and apparel, kids’ footwear, industrial work boots and apparel, and uniform shoes and boots. As of the end of 2024, the company generated sales from roughly 170 countries and territories. This makes it a truly global player. In addition to owning some of its own brands, it also licenses brands.

This is the kind of candidate that I would typically be drawn to. But in late October of last year, even as financial performance was coming in strong, I reaffirmed the business as a ‘hold’ candidate. This was driven not only by valuation, but by other factors as well, such as the risk that tariffs posed, the absence of detailed guidance from management, and broader economic uncertainty. Since then, the stock has dropped, falling 16.3% while the S&P 500 is up 1.4%. And even though that has been painful, I am honestly getting closer to upgrading the stock. Relative to other similar firms, it is a bit on the cheap end of the spectrum. And if we see a further decline, an upgrade will be unavoidable absent a big change in fundamentals. For now, however, I believe that maintaining it as a ‘hold’ is the right choice.

Not A Good Fit… Yet

The only new data that investors have regarding Wolverine World Wide covers through the third quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. That window of time saw revenue come in strong at $470.3 million. That's an increase of 6.8% over the $440.2 million the company reported a year earlier. That rise in revenue brought with it a slight increase in profitability, with net income climbing from $23.2 million to $25.1 million. Other profitability metrics were mixed. For instance, operating cash flow fell precipitously from $108.2 million to $33 million. Even if we strip out changes in working capital, we would get a fall from $71 million to $37.2 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business did increase, growing from $33.2 million to $48.1 million.

The first nine months of 2025 saw similar mixed performance. However, on the whole, I would say that window of time was better than the third quarter on its own. Revenue, profits, and two of the three cash flow metrics for the company, all improved year over year. The only thing that worsened was operating cash flow. To really understand all that is going on, however, we would be best off digging into each of the operating segments that the company has. Although there is a small ‘other’ segment that accounts for a small portion of revenue, broadly speaking, there are only two operating segments that really have a big impact on the business.

The most significant of these is the Active Group, which focuses on its Merrell footwear and apparel, Saucony footwear and apparel, Sweaty Betty activewear, and its Chaco footwear products. According to management, revenue for this segment amounted to $352.8 million for the third quarter of 2025. That's 10.7% above the $318.7 million the segment was responsible for the previous year. This expansion is multifaceted, with some of it being brand-specific and some of it being driven by growth in key markets. Specifically, for instance, the company has talked about strong sales growth in the US and EMEA (Europe, Middle East, and Africa) regions.

The firm is also actively investing in launching new product lines. One example of this would be the ProGrid Omni 9 while another is Ride Millennium. This does not mean that there isn't some weakness. For instance, Sweaty Betty reported a decline in revenue because of soft consumer demand in the US direct to consumer and wholesale channels. Lower close out sales also proved to be a problem. And on top of this, a reduction in close out sales and soft consumer demand negatively affected revenue for its Chaco brand line. Fortunately, the rise in revenue here brought with it higher profits. Operating income grew from $53.3 million to $65.4 million.

By comparison, the Work Group hasn't fared as well. This involves its famous Wolverine footwear and apparel brand, as well as others such as Cat, Bates, Harley-Davidson, and HYTEST. Revenue for this segment actually dropped from $109.1 million to $105.9 million year over year. This doesn't actually surprise me. The economic climate in the US is worsening, which wouldn't necessarily lead to a reduction in demand when it comes to work related footwear and apparel. And on top of this, the company was negatively impacted to the tune of $1.3 million from the Harley-Davidson brand, which as I have detailed in prior articles is experiencing challenges because younger consumers prefer smaller and more affordable bikes. The good news is that, even though revenue worsened, profitability improved, growing from $16.3 million to $19.8 million. Management chalked this up to a higher mix of full price sales, a reduction in promotional activity, price increases that the company implemented, and supply chain cost initiatives that the business has been pushing for. It does appear as though certain tariff mitigation strategies are helping. So that's great to see.

When it comes to the 2025 fiscal year as a whole, management believes that revenue will have totaled between $1.855 billion and $1.87 billion. That would compare to the $1.76 billion the company reported for 2024. They also expect net profits of between $105 million and $109 million. For context, in the previous year, net income was only $47.9 million. But to be fair, profitability has long been volatile for this prospect. If we project out results based on what the company has seen already for 2025, we would get adjusted operating cash flow of about $138.1 million and EBITDA of $188.9 million. Those would both be improvements over what was seen in the previous year.

Armed with these estimates, and also using the results of the company achieved for 2024, we can see in the chart above how the stock is priced at the moment. Honestly, these multiples aren't bad. They just aren't great, either. Relative to other similar firms, it is worth noting that the stock is a tad bit on the cheap end of the spectrum. In the chart below, you can see precisely what I mean. On a price to earnings basis, only one of the five institutions that I compared it to ended up being cheaper than it is. And two of them were cheaper when it came to the other valuation approaches. This means that, relatively speaking, the company is looking decent. But as a value investor who is looking to outperform the market, I prefer a larger discount than what we see here, especially in light of recent mixed bottom-line results.

The good news is that management is working hard to capture additional market share. This includes pushing its Merrell and Saucony brand lines that the company described as performing strongly. They specifically mentioned in their third quarter earnings call that they are capturing market share here. What's really exciting to me is Saucony, which the company said is experiencing door count penetration of less than 25% of what it believes to be its potential. They expect the first half of 2026 to see greater performance on this basis and what we've seen in the first half of 2025. This does indicate that 2026 is likely to see some strength for the business relative to 2024. And if the stock price remains depressed, while fundamentals improve, that could certainly change my assessment of the picture.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I would argue that Wolverine World Wide is certainly worthy of attention. It's not a company that I feel comfortable upgrading just yet. But it's getting awfully close. If we do see some continued improvements from a fundamental standpoint, and/or we see the stock price fall further, an upgrade could be in store. But for now, I think that maintaining it as a ‘hold’ candidate is the right choice.

data.title: Hilton Worldwide Holdings: The Bull Case Remains Compelling
data.description:
Shares of Hilton Worldwide Holdings (HLT) have delivered a total return of 27% since my previous piece, Hilton Worldwide: 5 Reasons Why The Stock Is Now A Strong Buy, was published on March 11, 2025.

The move higher in Hilton shares has been driven by solid operating performance resulting in strong EPS growth. Additionally, the stock has experienced modest valuation multiple expansion. I believe the company's growth outlook remains strong and valuation remains attractive. Additionally, I view the company's business as being fairly insulated from AI disruption and Trump disruption risks. For these reasons, I am reiterating my Strong Buy rating on the stock.

Strong Operating Performance Despite Macro Challenges

The operating environment for the hotel business has been somewhat challenging of late. In the time since my previous piece, significant tariffs have come into force in the U.S., the U.S. government experienced its longest shutdown in history, and international travel to the U.S. declined in 2025. Despite these challenges, Hilton has still been able to deliver solid operating performance. In Q3 2025, Hilton noted that it expects to deliver FY 2025 system-wide RevPAR growth of 0% to 1%, which represents only a slight decline from the company's long-term target of 2%-4% annual RevPAR growth. Moreover, the company also continues to execute on its new room growth plans. Q3 2025 net unit growth came in at 6.5% relative to the same period a year ago, which is in line with the company's long-term new unit growth target.

As shown by the chart below, despite significant macro-driven challenges, consensus EPS estimates for FY 2025 have increased slightly from the level at the time of my previous piece. The stability of the company's earnings power speaks to the strength of its business model and focus on using new unit growth to drive EPS growth. At the time of my previous piece, Hilton had a development pipeline of 499,000 rooms, which has now increased to 515,000 rooms.

2026 Outlook

While the company has not yet released FY 2026 guidance, management noted on the company's Q3 calls that it feels incrementally better about the setup for 2026 compared to 2025. I agree with this view and expect business conditions in the U.S. to improve significantly relative to 2025. Consensus estimates currently call for the company to deliver FY 2026 EPS of $9.14, which represents 13.2% growth relative to the current consensus FY 2025 EPS estimate of $8.07.

I view the 13.2% EPS growth estimate for FY 2026 as reasonable given the company's continued momentum in its development of new units. New unit growth should allow Hilton to drive 6%-7% EPS growth, while a return to a more normal U.S. travel environment should help RevPAR growth come in closer to the company's 2%-4% long-term target. A third important driver of EPS growth is the company's share repurchase program. On January 14, 2026, Hilton announced that its Board had approved an increase of $3.5 billion to the company's share repurchase program, bringing the total authorization to $4.6 billion. Hilton currently has a market cap of $70 billion, and thus the repurchase authorization represents roughly 6.5% of shares outstanding. A pace of repurchases similar to recent history would suggest Hilton could complete more than $3.2 billion of purchases in FY 2026, which could drive an incremental 4.5% increase in EPS.

Taken together, assuming a 4.5% benefit from share repurchases, 6.5% new unit growth, and RevPAR of 3%, it would result in annual EPS growth of 14%, which is slightly better than consensus estimates. Additionally, the company should be able to drive incremental additional EPS growth through increasing franchise fees as contracts reset and the 5% in-place rate remains below the 5.6% steady-state rate. For these reasons, I believe Hilton is in a strong position to meet or exceed consensus EPS estimates for FY 2026.

Valuation Update

Hilton shares currently trade at 33x consensus FY 2026 EPS. Comparably, at the time of my previous piece, Hilton had been trading at 31x consensus forward EPS. Thus, Hilton has experienced modest valuation multiple expansion since the time of my previous piece. However, it is important to note that the broader market has also experienced modest valuation multiple expansion over the same time period. At the time of my prior piece, the S&P 500 had been trading at 21x consensus forward earnings, and it now trades at 22.3x consensus forward earnings. Thus, on a relative basis, Hilton has only experienced a very small valuation premium increase relative to the broader market. I continue to believe that Hilton has better long-term growth prospects (as I see potential for 13%+ EPS growth for the next decade), while I expect the S&P 500 to revert back to historical norms of high single-digit EPS growth in the coming years (following above-trend growth in 2026).

Hilton continues to trade at a moderate premium relative to its own historical average forward P/E multiple. This was the case at the time of my prior piece, and I continue to believe this premium is justified given the company's strong pipeline and increasingly resilient and geographically diversified earnings power.

Hilton shares continue to trade at a modest premium to peers Marriott and IHG. Hilton's valuation premium relative to these two peers has remained fairly stable since the time of my previous piece, and I continue to view Hilton's premium as reasonable given its stronger medium-term growth prospects. For context, Hilton's development pipeline share is 3.7x its existing share, while the metric is 2.5x for Marriott and 3x for IHG. Hyatt has better near-term EPS growth prospects and continues to trade at a substantial premium to Hilton.

Overall, I continue to view Hilton's valuation as reasonable and do not expect a sharp drop in the stock's forward P/E. That said, I do see more limited scope for valuation multiple expansion now than was the case at the time of my prior piece. The reason for this is that Hilton's multiple has already experienced modest multiple expansion relative to where the stock was trading at the time of my previous piece.

Insulated From AI And Trump Risks

The risks related to disruption from AI have spread to an increasingly wide range of companies. One sector that has come under significant selling pressure in recent months is the software sector amid investor concern that AI could pose a serious risk to these companies given the potential for decreased user count for software licenses. Another company that has seen a negative impact related to AI risk is Netflix, as AI has the potential to result in much lower content production costs, which could erode the company's moat due to its massive content spending advantage relative to peers. In terms of the travel industry, shares of Booking Holdings have recently been under pressure amid worries that AI could increase disintermediation risk as AI agents would be able to book directly with hotels and airlines. I see relatively limited AI risk to Hilton's business model and view the company as a potential beneficiary. One reason for this is that AI, in the long run, has the potential to make Hilton more efficient, resulting in a reduction of operating expenses and potential margin expansion. Moreover, to the extent AI platforms result in some disintermediation of online travel agencies (“OTAs”), Hilton could benefit due to incrementally better pricing due to more direct bookings.

President Trump's drive to improve affordability has impacted an increasing number of companies. Impacted companies include drug companies that have been forced to agree to significant pricing concessions, banks and credit card companies that have the potential to face significant headwinds related to Trump's drive to cap credit card interest at 10%, and independent power producers who stand to lose if Trump's desired cap on how much they can charge comes into effect. I believe that Trump's affordability push could expand to other sectors as well. However, I believe the hotel business is more immune to Trump-related affordability push risks, as travel tends to be a more discretionary expense and lower profile in nature as it relates to the affordability discussion. Moreover, the market is very diverse, with prices being set in each individual market. Additionally, I believe Hilton's risk related to a potential increase in tariffs is also fairly limited as the company's business is not based on physical goods. Given these factors, I believe that Hilton is more immune to Trump risk relative to many other sectors, which should be a positive for the stock in the near term.

Risks To Consider

The main near-term risk to the Hilton bull case would be a prolonged economic downturn. Travel tends to be highly discretionary in nature, and in the event of a sharp economic contraction, RevPAR would likely fall significantly for Hilton. Additionally, I would expect new unit growth and pipeline growth to slow in the event of a prolonged economic downturn. I do not expect an economic downturn in the near term and believe that lower interest rates in the U.S. could fuel a stronger-than-expected economy in the near term. That said, I would view any significant sell-off in Hilton shares related to macroeconomic risks as a long-term buying opportunity.

Conclusion

Hilton shares have delivered strong results since my previous Strong Buy piece was published. The company has delivered solid earnings growth despite a challenging macro backdrop. I expect the company to deliver another year of strong earnings growth in 2026, which has the potential to drive above-market returns for the stock.

Hilton shares have experienced modest valuation multiple expansion since the time of my prior piece, but I continue to view the stock's valuation as attractive relative to the broader market given strong long-term growth prospects. Additionally, I view the company as being fairly insulated from AI and Trump-related risks. For these reasons, I am reiterating my Strong Buy rating on the stock and believe that the bull case remains compelling.
